questions.question: A team wants to browse a hub of pre-trained foundation models and pre-built ML solutions that they can quickly deploy onto SageMaker infrastructure. Which SageMaker capability provides this?

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Role Manager, which defines persona-based access.
choices.explanation: Role Manager helps define role-based access for ML personas. It is not a model hub.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor, which detects production drift.
choices.explanation: Model Monitor watches deployed models for drift. It does not provide a catalog of pre-built models and solutions.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart, a hub of pre-built models and solutions.
choices.explanation: SageMaker JumpStart is a model hub offering pre-trained foundation models and pre-built ML solutions that can be deployed quickly onto SageMaker, accelerating getting started.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Clarify, which detects bias and explains models.
choices.explanation: Clarify focuses on bias detection and explainability. It is not a model hub for deploying pre-built models.

questions.question: An insurance company wants to automatically sort incoming documents into their own internal categories that do not match any generic, pre-built label set. They want a managed NLP service they can train on their categories. Which capability fits?

choices.text: Amazon Polly with neural voices.
choices.explanation: Amazon Polly produces speech from text. It cannot classify documents into custom categories.

choices.text: Amazon Transcribe custom vocabularies.
choices.explanation: Transcribe custom vocabularies improve speech-to-text accuracy for specific words. They do not classify text documents into categories.

choices.text: Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels.
choices.explanation: Rekognition Custom Labels trains custom image recognition models. The task is text document classification, which is Comprehend's domain, not image analysis.

choices.text: Amazon Comprehend custom classification, for your own categories.
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend supports custom classification, letting you train a model on your own labeled examples to sort documents into business-specific categories, plus custom entity recognition for domain-specific entities.

questions.question: A team is adjusting inference parameters and asks which parameters primarily influence the creativity and randomness of a model's text output without being the main drivers of latency or per-token pricing. Which statement is accurate?

choices.text: Top K is the single largest driver of inference latency.
choices.explanation: Latency is driven mainly by model size and type and the number of input and output tokens. Top K is not a major latency driver.

choices.text: Top P determines the size of the model's context window.
choices.explanation: The context window is a fixed model property describing how many tokens it can process. Top P only limits the probability mass of candidate tokens during generation.

choices.text: Temperature directly sets the per-token price charged by the model.
choices.explanation: Pricing is driven by input/output token counts, model size, and provider, not by the temperature setting. Temperature only affects output randomness.

choices.text: Temperature, Top P, and Top K affect randomness, not mainly cost or latency.
choices.explanation: Temperature, Top P, and Top K shape how random or focused the output is. They are not primary drivers of latency or pricing; cost is driven mainly by input and output tokens and model choice, and latency by model size and token counts.

questions.question: A security team wants to scan EC2 instances, container images in Amazon ECR, and Lambda functions for software vulnerabilities and CVE-based risks. Which AWS service should they use?

choices.text: Amazon Macie, which discovers sensitive data in S3.
choices.explanation: Amazon Macie discovers and classifies sensitive data in S3. It does not scan workloads for software vulnerabilities.

choices.text: Amazon Comprehend, which analyzes text.
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend is an NLP service. It has no vulnerability scanning capability.

choices.text: Amazon Inspector, which scans EC2, ECR images, and Lambda.
choices.explanation: Amazon Inspector automatically scans EC2 instances, ECR container images, and Lambda functions for package vulnerabilities and CVE-based risks, and integrates with Security Hub and EventBridge.

choices.text: AWS CloudTrail, which logs API activity.
choices.explanation: CloudTrail records API calls for auditing. It does not perform vulnerability scanning.

questions.question: A company must score a very large dataset of records overnight, where immediate per-record responses are not required and processing them together is more efficient. Which inference approach is most appropriate?

choices.text: Edge inference on a disconnected device.
choices.explanation: Edge inference runs predictions locally on devices for low-latency, offline, or privacy-sensitive needs. It does not fit a centralized large overnight batch scoring job.

choices.text: Batch inference, which processes large volumes together offline.
choices.explanation: Batch inference (or batch transform) processes large volumes of data together and is ideal when immediate responses are not needed and efficiency matters, such as overnight scoring of a large dataset.

choices.text: Real-time inference, which returns low-latency responses per request.
choices.explanation: Real-time inference is optimized for immediate, per-request responses. It is unnecessary and less efficient for a large overnight batch job.

choices.text: Interactive streaming inference for a chatbot.
choices.explanation: Streaming inference suits interactive, token-by-token chatbot responses. It is not appropriate for bulk offline scoring.

questions.question: An ML governance lead wants standardized documentation for each model describing its intended use, risk rating, training details, and limitations. Which Amazon SageMaker capability provides this documentation?

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store, which stores features.
choices.explanation: Feature Store stores and reuses features. It does not document model intended use and risk.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler, which prepares data.
choices.explanation: Data Wrangler is for data preparation. It is not a model documentation tool.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Pipelines, which orchestrate ML workflows.
choices.explanation: Pipelines automate ML workflow steps. They do not serve as standardized model documentation.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Cards, documenting a model’s use and risk.
choices.explanation: SageMaker Model Cards capture model documentation such as intended use, risk, training details, and limitations, supporting governance and transparency across the model lifecycle.

questions.question: An attacker submits malicious examples into a model's training data to corrupt what the model learns. Which generative AI risk does this describe, and what helps mitigate it?

choices.text: Hallucination; mitigate it by increasing temperature.
choices.explanation: Hallucination is plausible-but-incorrect output, not corruption of training data. Increasing temperature would not mitigate it and could make outputs less reliable.

choices.text: Data poisoning; mitigate with data validation and trusted sources.
choices.explanation: Data poisoning is the injection of malicious data into the training set to corrupt model behavior. Mitigations include validating data, using trusted sources, and monitoring for anomalies.

choices.text: Overfitting; mitigate it by making the model larger.
choices.explanation: Overfitting is a generalization problem from training, not a deliberate attack on training data. Making the model larger can worsen overfitting, so this is incorrect.

choices.text: Prompt leaking; mitigate it by adding more training epochs.
choices.explanation: Prompt leaking exposes hidden prompts or sensitive context at inference time. It is unrelated to injecting malicious training data, and more epochs would not address it.

questions.question: A nontechnical user wants to experiment with building generative AI apps in a playground powered by Amazon Bedrock, with no coding and without needing an AWS account. Which offering fits?

choices.text: AWS CloudFormation, an infrastructure-as-code service.
choices.explanation: CloudFormation provisions infrastructure with templates. It is not a no-code GenAI app playground.

choices.text: PartyRock, a no-code GenAI app playground powered by Bedrock.
choices.explanation: PartyRock is a generative AI app-building playground powered by Amazon Bedrock that requires no coding and no AWS account, making it ideal for experimenting with foundation models and app ideas.

choices.text: Amazon SageMaker Studio, a full ML development environment.
choices.explanation: SageMaker Studio is a professional ML development interface that requires an AWS account and technical skills. It is not the no-account playground described.

choices.text: AWS Lambda, a serverless compute service.
choices.explanation: Lambda runs serverless functions and requires an AWS account and code. It is not a no-code experimentation playground.

questions.question: A team is evaluating a regression model that predicts house prices and wants a metric that penalizes larger errors more heavily. Which metric fits this requirement?

choices.text: Recall, the proportion of actual positives found.
choices.explanation: Recall is a classification metric. It is not used to measure error magnitude in a regression model.

choices.text: F1 score, the balance of precision and recall.
choices.explanation: F1 score balances precision and recall for classification. It does not measure regression error and does not emphasize larger errors.

choices.text: Accuracy, the ratio of correct classifications.
choices.explanation: Accuracy is a classification metric for discrete categories. It does not apply to predicting a continuous house price.

choices.text: RMSE, which penalizes larger errors more heavily.
choices.explanation: RMSE squares the errors before averaging and taking the root, so larger errors contribute disproportionately. This makes RMSE appropriate when large errors are especially undesirable.

questions.question: A generative AI application must prevent sensitive personal data from appearing in model responses to users. Which Amazon Bedrock capability can redact personally identifiable information (PII) in prompts and responses?

choices.text: Amazon Bedrock batch inference.
choices.explanation: Batch inference processes many predictions efficiently. It provides no PII redaction or content filtering.

choices.text: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases.
choices.explanation: Knowledge Bases support RAG by retrieving relevant context. They ground answers in data but do not provide PII redaction; Guardrails handle that.

choices.text: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails, which can redact PII and filter content.
choices.explanation: Bedrock Guardrails can detect and redact PII in prompts and responses, block denied topics, and filter harmful content, directly addressing the requirement to keep sensitive data out of responses.

choices.text: Amazon Bedrock provisioned throughput.
choices.explanation: Provisioned throughput reserves model capacity for performance. It does not redact PII or filter content.
